titleStmt.title: The Secretary of State to the Embassy in France

head.hi: The Secretary of State to the Embassy in France

div.p:
5207. Eyes only for Jessup and Bohlen. Because of several indications including Lie’s suggestions to Gross (relayed to Paris as Deptel 4831)See footnote 2, p. 248. we suspect that Gromyko may be authorized to discuss privately possibilities of a negotiated settlement of Korean conflict.
At the State–JCS meeting on the next day, Mr. Nitze made the following statement:
“There are indications that the Soviet Union wants to negotiate. Zinchenko—who is one of the Soviet citizens on the U.N. Secretariat—suggested to Lie that Gromyko and Jessup should discuss this problem in Paris. It seems to us that he would not have made this suggestion unless the Soviet Union sincerely desired to negotiate.” (State–JCS Meeting, April 4, 1951; Lot File 64D563, Box 728) In view of delicacy of subject and intervening developments we believe that exploration of Soviet attitude shld begin one level below Gromyko.

Therefore, we suggest that BohlenCharles E. Bohlen, Minister at the American Embassy in Paris, was a member of the U.S. Delegation headed by Mr. Jessup to the Deputies meetings currently underway in Paris (see vol. iii, Part 1, pp. 1086 ff.). go on a fishing expedition with Lavrentiev.A. I. Lavrentiev, Soviet Deputy Foreign Minister, was a member of the Soviet Delegation headed by Mr. Gromyko to the Deputies meetings. If no objection perceived will he please contrive to meet
alone with Lavrentiev and create an opportunity for Lavrentiev to unburden himself of Soviet reactions to Korean situation and, particularly, Soviet thinking on a settlement of the Korean conflict.
These instructions are of course designed only to draw out Soviet position and in no way to commit ourselves to anything beyond already announced aims of UN in Korea.

As this would be an informal approach, simply giving the Russians an opportunity to speak their piece, you shld not inform Davies and Parodi of the projected meeting. After Bohlen has had the conversation and if there is any development of the situation we can then consider their desirability of informing British and French.
